policy_statement: By 2025, implement management/administration plans for at least 40% of MPAs created before 2020 through monitoring, control, community involvement, and threat control programs (I11b).
mechanism_description: It will implement management or administration plans for marine protected areas through monitoring, control, community involvement, and threat control programs to reduce ecosystem threats and preserve marine ecosystems and blue-carbon storage.
primary_causal_pathway: Ecosystem protection/management → ↓ habitat degradation + ↑ ecosystem integrity → ↑/maintained carbon sinks + ↓ carbon stock loss
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) What changes: Human management of marine protected areas—monitoring, patrol/set, regulated use, threat abatement (e.g., illegal harvesting, pollution, destructive practices), and community co-management.  
2) Mediating process: Reduced physical disturbance and ecological stress preserves habitat structure and sediment stability, maintaining primary production and long-term carbon burial/storage in vegetated coastal systems and associated food webs (“blue carbon”).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased or maintained net carbon sequestration and avoided GHG emissions that would occur if habitats are degraded/converted (loss of biomass and sediment carbon).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is conserving/restoring marine and coastal ecosystems so they continue sequestering and storing blue carbon (and avoid emissions from ecosystem degradation).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Ecosystem protection/management leads to preserved/enhanced blue-carbon sinks and avoided carbon-stock loss.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While MPAs can also improve climate resilience (e.g., coastal protection, fisheries stability), the mechanism as defined explicitly targets ecosystem threat reduction and blue-carbon preservation—an NbS sink/stock pathway rather than energy/material efficiency or hazard-exposure reduction.  
3) Edge cases applied: If an MPA plan is framed mainly for disaster risk reduction (wave attenuation) it could lean Adaptation, but under this canonical mechanism the defining feature is carbon/ecosystem preservation, keeping NbS dominant.  
4) Applies to ALL instances: Any policy fitting “MPA management → ecosystem/blue carbon preservation” has its principal climate effect through conserving natural carbon stocks/sinks in marine/coastal ecosystems.  
5) Secondary subtype deferred: Whether the policy yields net sequestration increases vs primarily avoids emissions, and which habitats (mangrove vs seagrass vs saltmarsh), requires policy-level/ecosystem evidence and is not assigned here.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If the protected area lacks blue-carbon habitats (e.g., offshore rocky reef without significant carbon burial) the carbon benefit may be smaller and biodiversity/adaptation co-benefits may dominate; however, the canonical mechanism explicitly references blue-carbon preservation, so classification remains Nature-Based Solutions.
key_indicators: “By 2025”; “at least 40% of the marine protected areas”; “management or administration plans … will be implemented”; “monitoring”; “control”; “community involvement”; “threat control programs”
co_benefits: Biodiversity conservation; fisheries sustainability; improved governance/compliance; coastal water quality benefits
instance_edge_case_notes: Could also be framed as a planning-strategy (implementation of management plans), but the explicit quantified coverage threshold and deadline make target-setting the closest fit.
